toolUseResult.oldString:
### Authoring surfaces expose both `label` and `kind`

Wherever a link can be created interactively, both fields are first-class inputs with the right control type for each:

- **CLI** (`nsg writ link …`) accepts `label` as a positional/named argument and `kind` as an optional flag. Both fields are documented in the command's help text. (The existing command already supports the equivalent shape under the old names; this commission carries the rename through and tightens the help text.)
- **Oculus link-creation UI** (when one is built — see Out of scope) presents `label` as a free text input and `kind` as a dropdown sourced from the kit-contributed registry (`listKinds()`). Operators don't type kind ids by hand.

Authoring with `kind` omitted creates a documentary link (`kind = null`); this is the casual-tagging path and remains supported on every authoring surface.

The Clerk writs page (`packages/plugins/clerk/pages/writs/index.html`) has an inline add-link form on every writ, with a target-id input and a link-type input (with autocomplete via `<datalist>`). So the existing UI is **incomplete with respect to the substrate** — it never exposed `meaning`/`kind` even though the substrate has supported it since the writ-link brief landed.

Updating it is in scope. Let me revise:

content.content:
date: 2026-04-17
    item: "Gave Astrolabe sage roles click-read access (nexus commit da0e460). Triggered by Sean noticing a dense click-referencing brief (.scratch/brief-spider-follows-gating.md) that the planner would receive as opaque tokens — non-negotiables citing 'full design at c-...', scope fences as 'captured at c-... (parked)', cross-commission conventions as 'convention captured at c-...'. Without click access the sage would re-open settled questions or pull parked concerns back into scope. Added 'ratchet' to astrolabe apparatus recommends (required for the loom's dependency-scoped permission validation to accept the new grant), added ratchet:read to all four sage role grants (sage-reader, sage-analyst, sage-writer, sage-reading-analyst), added click-extract/show/tree/list to each role's tool list in the prompt, added a status-aware 'Click references' section to each prompt (concluded→pre-empted decision with click id in rationale, parked→scope fence, live→surface as dependency, dropped→context only — ties into the existing razor pre-emption rule). Updated supportkit tests, README roles table, and clarified an instrumentarium test comment that claimed to mirror the exact sage permissions. All 135 astrolabe + 120 tools + 89 loom tests pass. Opened click c-mo3574k9 to track the broader pattern of autonomous-role click access; the sage grant is the first slice but the artificer will hit the same opaque-token problem when a brief with click refs is commissioned to it."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

  - session: c6dd7223-e4c7-4735-9544-8f1b564d7662
    date: 2026-04-17
    item: "Sage-writer pivot + click-tree disposition (nexus commit 260f5cf). Sean corrected the writer instruction I'd just shipped: the writer should NOT preserve click ids in the generated brief, because the consumers of the brief (artificer, reviewers) don't have click access — a click id in the final brief is a dead pointer. The writer should instead resolve every click reference it encounters (as input), extract the substantive content, and inline the relevant portions in its own prose so the generated brief is self-contained. Clicks are the sage's source material; the brief is the delivered artifact. This reframes the guild's design: internal artifacts (scope, decisions, observations) may reference clicks because the patron consumes them and has click access; external artifacts (the generated brief) must stand alone. Click-tree follow-on: dropped the artificer child click I'd just opened (c-mo35j1dn) as moot — its driver was 'consumers receive briefs with click refs' and that driver is now gone. Opened+concluded c-mo35nzja as the sage-slice child under c-mo3574k9 for durable record. Assessed other roles (reviewer, mender, engine sessions) and none have a concrete click-access driver post-pivot, so didn't open speculative children. Concluded the parent c-mo3574k9 with the pattern-level summary; re-open per-role when a real driver emerges."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

- session: c6dd7223-e4c7-4735-9544-8f1b564d7662
    date: 2026-04-17
    item: "Sage-writer pivot + click-tree disposition (nexus commit 260f5cf). Sean corrected the writer instruction I'd just shipped: the writer should NOT preserve click ids in the generated brief, because the consumers of the brief (artificer, reviewers) don't have click access — a click id in the final brief is a dead pointer. The writer should instead resolve every click reference it encounters (as input), extract the substantive content, and inline the relevant portions in its own prose so the generated brief is self-contained. Clicks are the sage's source material; the brief is the delivered artifact. This reframes the guild's design: internal artifacts (scope, decisions, observations) may reference clicks because the patron consumes them and has click access; external artifacts (the generated brief) must stand alone. Click-tree follow-on: dropped the artificer child click I'd just opened (c-mo35j1dn) as moot — its driver was 'consumers receive briefs with click refs' and that driver is now gone. Opened+concluded c-mo35nzja as the sage-slice child under c-mo3574k9 for durable record. Assessed other roles (reviewer, mender, engine sessions) and none have a concrete click-access driver post-pivot, so didn't open speculative children. Concluded the parent c-mo3574k9 with the pattern-level summary; re-open per-role when a real driver emerges."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

  - session: a4be8f54-bef6-45aa-9cd5-21a5a6ae196a
    date: 2026-04-17
    item: "Spider dispatch-gating design session and brief drafting (commission w-mo35s0fo). Worked through the remaining live sub-questions under the spider parent click (c-mo2e88aw): composition (conjunctive), re-evaluation trigger (Spider's existing poll), cycle handling (Spider detects, Clerk stays thin), auto-unstick on retry (yes), stuck-reason text format. Sean caught a real design tension I'd missed — freeform stuck_reason text doesn't give Spider a way to identify which stuck transitions it owns; pivoted to a Kubernetes-style spec/status convention (status.<pluginId> per runtime object, plugins write only their own slot, last-writer-wins, status mutations emit CDC). First consumer: Spider records gate state and stuckCause in status.spider. Established two further system-wide naming decisions under patron pressure: (1) dots not colons as the separator for plugin-namespaced ids (link kinds, role contributions, event patterns) — the existing link-meaning substrate using colons was the outlier; (2) renamed link-substrate fields type→label and semanticMeaning→kind, with the surrounding vocabulary (linkMeanings→linkKinds, MeaningDoc→KindDoc, listMeanings→listKinds) following in lockstep. Captured failures in the session worth noting: (a) initially overstepped by unilaterally 'correcting' spider.follows to spider:follows when I noticed the substrate's id-format mismatch — Sean called it out; the right move was surfacing the conflict, not deciding which side bends; (b) first brief draft drifted into spec territory with file:line refs, exact API signatures, and per-test-case enumeration — Sean reframed as 'pre-Astrolabe brief documents INTENT and NON-NEGOTIABLE DECISIONS, not implementation'; rewrote in the right register (~140 lines, no §1/§2 implementation plan). Brief covers Spider gating + status convention + naming sweep + authoring-surface updates (CLI flag rename, completing the existing inline add-link form on the writs page to expose kind as a registry-sourced dropdown). Database/data migration explicitly excluded — Sean handles out of band. Eight clicks concluded, two parked, two opened-as-supporting-decisions during the session."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

content.text:
Committed. Quick session recap:

**Dispatched:** w-mo35s0fo-1a1e3cd285bc — Spider dispatch gating via `spider.follows`

**Design space resolved (eight clicks concluded, two parked):**
- Composition: conjunctive
- Re-evaluation: Spider's existing poll
- Cycle handling: Spider detects, Clerk stays thin
- Auto-unstick on retry: yes, automatic
- Stuck-reason: free text + structured provenance via `status.spider`
- Lifecycle: gating happens entirely from `open`
- Provenance: `status.spider.stuckCause` discriminates Spider-authored stucks

**Substrate adjustments bundled in:**
- K8s-style status convention for runtime objects
- Naming sweep: `type→label`, `semanticMeaning→kind`, vocabulary follows in lockstep
- Separator change: `:` → `.` for plugin-namespaced ids (system-wide convention)

**Authoring surfaces extended:** CLI flag rename + completing the existing inline add-link form on the writs page (kind dropdown sourced from `listKinds()`).

**Visibility-gap concern parked at c-mo301yp9** for future Oculus surfacing.

Two failures worth flagging from my side:
1. I unilaterally "fixed" `spider.follows` to `spider:follows` early on instead of surfacing the conflict to you.
2. First brief draft drifted into spec territory; you reframed it as intent-only and I rewrote.

Both are useful corrections to internalize. Standing down on this thread.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /workspace/nexus-mk2/.claude/skills/wrap-up

# Wrap-Up: Complete Work and Generate Session Summary

## Generate Summary

Generate a structured summary of the current session. This summary serves one audience:

**The Scribe** — as input for session notes and published material.

Session-to-session continuity is now carried by **clicks** (see Coco's agent file and the clicks skill). Before generating the wrap-up summary, conclude or park any clicks touched this session — the click tree is what the next Coco session will read for orientation, not this wrap-up file.

<expected-format>
# Session Summary

## What we did

- Bulleted list of concrete actions taken (files created/modified, decisions made, commits pushed). Reference specific files and commit hashes.

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- What was decided, including explicit "we chose NOT to" decisions. Brief rationale for each.

## Next steps & open questions

- Concrete actions for the next session or for agents to pick up.
- Open questions which were raised but not answered

### Notable moments

Capture quotes, insights, or exchanges that stand out — either for system design or for the research/experiment narrative.

For each notable moment:
- Include **direct quotes from Sean** when available
- Flag **why it's notable** (e.g., "shifted the project's core abstraction", "surfaced a tension between X and Y", "revealed an assumption about how agents should work")
- Prioritize moments where the human changed direction, challenged an assumption, or articulated something new about the project's purpose

### Experiment observations

Flag anything relevant to active experiments. Omit this section if nothing applies.

<expected-format>

## Complete Work

After wrapping up, **ALWAYS**:

- Commit any changes you have made, if any
- Push main to the remote

## Reminders

Check for things Sean should be nudged about:

- **Ethnographer interview:** Check the most recent interview file in `experiments/ethnography/interviews/` (files named `YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSS.md`). If the most recent one is more than a day old — or if none exist yet — remind Sean to run an ethnographer session. Keep it light: *"It's been a couple days since your last ethnographer interview — might be a good time to check in."*

## Guidelines

- Keep the whole summary under ~500 words. Brevity is a feature.
- Do not editorialize beyond the "why it's notable" flags.
- Write the summary to `experiments/ethnography/session-notes/new/<timestamp>.md`. where `<timestamp>` is the current date and time formatted as `YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSS.md` (use the actual current time — don't round to the nearest hour). Each session gets its own file — never overwrite a previous one. This file is for the ethnographer and must NOT be auto-deleted — it accumulates until the ethnographer reviews it.
- Commit all `session-notes`, and `experiments/data/transcripts/` changes to git and push those changes. The transcripts directory may contain files from concurrent sessions — commit whatever is there.
- In addition to writing the summary, share it in the chat for review and inclusion in session transcripts

# Session Summary

## What we did

- Completed the design subtree for Spider dispatch gating (parent click `c-mo2e88aw`). Resolved the five remaining sub-questions: composition (conjunctive), re-evaluation trigger (Spider's existing poll), cycle handling (Spider detects, Clerk stays thin), auto-unstick on retry (yes), stuck-reason text format.
- Pivoted mid-session to introduce a Kubernetes-style status convention for runtime objects after Sean caught a provenance gap in the freeform stuck-reason approach. Six conclusions landed under a new convention parent click `c-mo33duvq`; one parked (typed contributions, deferred).
- Established a system-wide naming convention: dots (not colons) as the separator for plugin-namespaced identifiers (`c-mo34644p`).
- Renamed link-substrate fields under patron pressure: `type → label`, `semanticMeaning → kind`, with surrounding vocabulary (`linkMeanings → linkKinds`, `MeaningDoc → KindDoc`, `listMeanings → listKinds`) following in lockstep (`c-mo34jdht`).
- Drafted, revised, and dispatched the brief as commission `w-mo35s0fo-1a1e3cd285bc` ("Spider Dispatch Gating via `spider.follows`").
- Added coco-log entry; commit `30d7c87` (Coco identity, signed with session id).

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- **Conjunctive composition for `spider.follows`** — all blockers must release.
- **Polling-based re-evaluation** — no new event-dispatch substrate.
- **Spider detects cycles, Clerk stays thin.** Hard error surfaces as stuck-with-cycle reason.
- **Auto-unstick on recovery** — operator's action *was* fixing the dependency; no second manual step.
- **Status convention** — K8s-style spec/status, plugin-namespaced slots, last-writer-wins, mutations emit CDC, persists across terminal states. Convention only (no enforcement). Typed contributions deferred.
- **Naming**: `label` + `kind` for the two link fields; dots not colons for plugin-namespaced ids.
- **Database/data migration** — explicitly excluded from the brief, handled out of band by the patron.
- **Oculus link-creation UI** — completing the existing inline add-link form on the writs page is in scope (kind dropdown sourced from `listKinds()`); a richer redesign is deferred.
- **Visibility-gap on `open` overload** — parked at `c-mo301yp9` for future Oculus surfacing work.

## Next steps & open questions

- Commission `w-mo35s0fo` is dispatched and in flight. Next interaction with this thread is when it returns for review.
- Open the visibility-gap (`c-mo301yp9`) when Oculus work picks up — `status.spider` will give that work concrete material to render.

### Notable moments

- **"why did you override me and decide on spider:follows, with the colon instead of period?"** — Sean caught me unilaterally "fixing" the meaning id when I noticed the substrate's id-format mismatch. The right move was surfacing the conflict, not deciding which side bends. Notable because it's a clean instance of the patron-emissary boundary in action: I'm supposed to *do* analytical work but *surface* decisions. Easy to drift into "I'll just decide and record it" when the answer feels obvious. Cascaded into the much better outcome: a system-wide separator convention.
- **"this is a pre-astrolabe brief so we need to document the INTENT and NON-NEGOTIABLE DECISIONS. But you get pretty far into the weeds on files and code-level specifics... which i think we are trying to minimize in our finished specs, let alone in the pre-spec briefs"** — Sean reframed brief-writing register. My first draft included file:line references, exact API signatures, per-test-case enumeration. Notable because it's a vocabulary clarification for the guild's role split: briefs (pre-Astrolabe) carry intent + non-negotiables; specs (Astrolabe's output) carry implementation; artificers carry code. I had been collapsing those layers.
- **"i basically favor simplicity anyway... why are you asking me this?!"** — light teasing on me asking permission for routine click ops. Reinforced the "keep click operations low-profile" directive — bookkeeping should be invisible most of the time.

## Reminder for Sean

It's been about ten days since your last ethnographer interview (2026-04-07) — might be a good time to check in.

file.content:
- **Experiment superseded or cancelled** — move it to Superseded with a one-line reason.

Update the index in the same commit as the experiment change. Don't let it drift.

## Collaborating on Documents

When collaborating on content (documents, philosophy, specs, plans), draft it in `.scratch/` first. This gives Sean a navigable file he can annotate and review in his editor, rather than trying to collaborate inline in chat. Once the content is finalized, move it to its permanent location. **When publishing a draft to its permanent location, delete the scratch file.** Don't let `.scratch/` accumulate stale drafts.

### Capturing Todos

When we table an item for later but want to preserve the decision or question, store it as a click (see below). These are not drafts heading toward a permanent location — they're parking spots for open questions we want to pick back up later.

### Transcript Capture

When Sean provides feedback on draft documents (via file edits, annotations, or out-of-band comments), restate a summary of that feedback in your chat response. Use Sean's direct words as much as possible. This "states it for the record" — ensuring the substance of the feedback appears in the transcript where Scribe can capture it, even when the collaboration itself happened in external files.

## Clicks

**Clicks** are your primary session-continuity mechanism — atomic decision-nodes managed by the Ratchet apparatus, organized in a tree. Each click captures one question or inquiry; when resolved, it records the conclusion. Clicks replace the earlier quest writ type.

When you need to create, view, transition, or conclude a click, invoke the **clicks skill** (`.claude/skills/clicks/SKILL.md`) for the full workflow and `nsg click` commands. At startup, scan the click tree with:

    nsg click tree --status live --status parked

Don't eagerly read every click. When the conversation turns toward a specific area, use `nsg click extract --id <id>` to load the full subtree as narrative context — that's the primary orientation command for diving into any line of inquiry. Use `nsg click show <id>` only for single-click inspection; **don't walk a subtree by calling `show` on each child** — that's what `extract` is for.

content.text:
Push done. Now to your message about the instruction update.

The natural home is the **Collaborating on Documents** section of `coco.md`, as a new subsection alongside "Capturing Todos" and "Transcript Capture." Here's my proposed addition:

---

**### Briefs: intent, not implementation**

Pre-Astrolabe briefs document **intent** and **non-negotiable decisions** — what the work accomplishes and which design choices the implementer cannot revisit. They do not document *how* to implement.

Specifically, briefs **do not** include:

- File paths, line numbers, or "in `foo.ts:123`, replace X with Y" instructions.
- Exact API signatures or full code blocks showing field-by-field schemas. (A short shape sketch is fine when it's the cleanest way to express intent; full type definitions are not.)
- Per-test-case enumeration. Test coverage scope is for the spec.
- Numbered §1/§2/§3 implementation plans. Briefs organize around design topics, not implementation steps.
- "Exit criteria" feature checklists or commodity constraints (lint passes, etc.) — those are table stakes, not brief content.

The role split:

- **Brief (Coco's draft)** — design intent, locked-in decisions, scope fences, references to the source clicks. Audience: the sage (Astrolabe), who will resolve click references and produce the spec.
- **Spec (Astrolabe's output)** — implementation plan, file-level changes, API signatures, test coverage. Audience: the implementing artificer.
- **Code (artificer's output)** — the actual implementation. Audience: future agents reading the codebase.

When tempted to add "in `clerk.ts:247-264`, change `id.indexOf(':')` to `id.indexOf('.')`" to a brief, ask: *is this the design intent, or am I doing the spec's job?* If it's the spec's job, leave it out — the sage will figure out the right files and lines from the intent.

A brief that drifts into spec territory leaks decisions that should have been left to the implementer's judgment, makes the brief brittle to refactoring (file:line refs go stale), and collapses the role boundary that the guild's organization depends on.

---

A few notes on placement and framing:

1. I put this alongside the existing "Capturing Todos" and "Transcript Capture" subsections under **Collaborating on Documents** because briefs *are* a kind of collaborative document we draft in `.scratch/`. That section already has the file-staging directive ("draft in `.scratch/` first... delete the scratch file when published"), and the new subsection extends it with the *content* discipline for briefs specifically.
2. The role-split table (brief / spec / code) makes the boundary concrete. If you want it shorter I can drop that and rely on "intent, not implementation" carrying the load.
3. I deliberately left out a length guideline (e.g., "briefs are typically <200 lines") because length is a symptom, not the discipline. Drift into spec is the real failure mode.

Want me to commit this as proposed, edit something first, or move it elsewhere (e.g., its own skill file under `.claude/skills/briefs/`)?

message.content: with completely eclude test cases and exit criteria.. is that the right choice? how much of that is about 'intent' vs 'implementation detail'?

content.text:
Fair pushback. I was being too binary. Let me actually work through it.

**Tests — what part is intent vs implementation?**

A test serves multiple purposes; only some of them are implementation:

- **"This scenario must be covered"** — intent. It's saying "if you don't think about this case, you've missed something about the design." Especially valuable for non-obvious edges (cycles, race-y recovery paths, conjunctive composition with mixed states).
- **Test file location, helper functions, mock setup** — pure implementation.
- **Exact assertion text** — implementation.

So my brief's enumeration that listed *scenarios* like "writ in a 3-cycle transitions to `stuck` with `cycleMembers` listing all three" — that's actually intent, not implementation. The mistake was prescribing file paths (`spider.test.ts`) and structuring it as "here are the tests to write."

For simple briefs, scenarios are obvious from the design text and listing them is redundant. For complex ones with subtle edges, an explicit "scenarios that must be covered" section captures observations that emerged during the design conversation — observations that might otherwise live only in clicks and get lost in translation.

**Exit criteria — same split:**

- **"After this lands, X behavior must be true"** — intent stated as acceptance. Useful for multi-part briefs where a checklist helps confirm coverage.
- **"File Y exists at path Z"** — implementation.
- **"Lint passes, all tests pass"** — table stakes, not real intent. Noise.

My original brief had a mix. The behavioral half was OK; the artifact-path and table-stakes half wasn't.

**Revised guidance:**

Drop "completely exclude tests/exit criteria" and replace with:

---

Briefs **may** include a *scenarios to verify* list when the design has non-obvious edges (cycles, recovery paths, composition rules) that the implementer might not naturally surface. Frame these as behavioral cases ("writ in a 3-cycle transitions to `stuck` with all members listed"), not as test prescriptions ("add a test in `spider.test.ts` that mocks..."). For simple briefs where scenarios are obvious from the design text, omit.

Briefs **may** include *behavioral exit criteria* — "after this lands, X must be true" — when the design has multiple parts and a checklist helps confirm coverage. Frame these as observable outcomes, not as artifact-existence checks.

Briefs **should not** include:

- Test file paths, helper names, mock setup, or assertion mechanics.
- Exit criteria that restate the design section verbatim.
- Exit criteria that name specific files or paths ("`docs/architecture/status-convention.md` exists").
- Commodity constraints ("lint passes," "all tests pass") — those are always true and add noise.

---

So the test/exit-criteria sections aren't categorically excluded — they're allowed but constrained to *behavioral* content, never *implementation* content. The line is intent vs how-to, not test/no-test.

Want me to swap this in for the bullet-list exclusion in the proposed instruction, then commit?

  edit [options] [id]                Edit a writ, updating its title, body,

  complete [options]                 Complete a writ, transitioning it from open
                                     to completed
  fail [options]                     Fail a writ, transitioning it from open or
                                     stuck to failed
  cancel [options] [id]              Cancel a writ, transitioning it from new,
                                     open, or stuck to cancelled
  publish [options] [id]             Publish a draft writ, transitioning it from
                                     new to open
  link [options]                     Link two writs with a typed relationship
  unlink [options]                   Remove a link between two writs
  link-meanings [options]            List registered link meanings
  link-meanings-show [options] [id]  Show a single link meaning by id
  types                              List available writ types for this guild
  help [command]                     display help for command

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# bin/commission.sh — Post a commission to the Clerk
#
# Posts a commission writ. The Spider picks it up and dispatches to an
# anima automatically. Data collection (commission artifacts, session
# records, commission log, quality scoring) is handled by the
# Laboratory apparatus running in the guild.
#
# What this script does:
#   1. Post commission to the Clerk → writ ID
#   2. Patch complexity into the commission log (if provided)
#   3. Report outcome
#
# What the Laboratory handles (via CDC, triggered automatically):
#   - Commission data dir, commission.md, review.md template
#   - Commission log skeleton entry
#   - Session records (timing, cost, tokens)
#   - Quality scoring on writ completion
#   - Auto-commits of all data artifacts
#
# What the Spider handles:
#   - Picking up ready writs and dispatching to an anima
#   - Full session lifecycle (accept → draft → summon → seal → push → complete/fail)
#
# Usage:
#   ./bin/commission.sh --codex <codex> [options] -- 'commission body'
#   ./bin/commission.sh --codex <codex> [options] -- @file.md
#
# Arguments:
#   --codex       Name of a registered codex (must be added via codex-add)
#   --complexity  Patron complexity estimate (Fibonacci: 1 2 3 5 8 13 21).
#                 If provided, updates the Laboratory's commission log entry.
#   --guild-path  Guild root directory (default: /workspace/vibers)
#   -- 'body'     Commission body text, or @<path> to read from file
#
# Title Extraction:
#   The title is extracted from the first line of the commission body.
#   Markdown header prefixes (# ## ### etc.) are stripped automatically.
#
# Prerequisites:
#   Guild must have clerk and laboratory plugins configured.
#
# Exit codes:
#   0 — commission posted successfully
#   1 — usage error or missing arguments
#   2 — commission post failed

set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
PROJECT_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
LOG_FILE="$PROJECT_ROOT/experiments/data/commission-log.yaml"

# nsg CLI invocation (TypeScript source, needs experimental flags)
NSG_CMD=(node --disable-warning=ExperimentalWarning --experimental-transform-types
  /workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli/src/cli.ts)

# ── Parse arguments ───────────────────────────────────────────

CODEX=""

---
description: Work with clicks — Coco's session-continuity mechanism. Invoke when opening, updating, resuming, or concluding a line of inquiry that should outlive the current session.
---

# Clicks — Session-Continuity Workflow

> NOTE: The environment should have a properly defined `GUILD_ROOT` allowing the `nsg` command to be used without `--guild-root`. If this is not the case, the guild root (at `/workspace/vibers`) can be appended to commands as needed.

A **click** is an atomic decision-node managed by the Ratchet apparatus. Each click captures one question or inquiry; when resolved, it records the conclusion. Clicks are organized in a tree — children are sub-questions of their parent. Together, the click tree forms a structured record of the reasoning and decisions that guide the guild's work.

Clicks are Coco's primary mechanism for session continuity, replacing the earlier quest writ type. Key differences from quests:

- **Goal is immutable.** Set at creation, never changes. If the framing is wrong, drop and create a new one.
- **Conclusion is write-once.** Set by `conclude` or `drop`, then frozen.
- **No body files.** Click goals are short (one sentence). Long-form exploration lives in session transcripts, joinable via session ID.
- **The tree is the product.** Value lives in structure (hierarchy, decomposition), not prose. Children are the todo list.
- **Four statuses:** `live | parked | concluded | dropped`. Simple, designed for inquiry.

## When to open a click

Open a click when a conversation or line of thought has enough substance to outlive the current session. Heuristics:

- Sean is exploring a design question across multiple turns
- You've tabled something with "let's come back to this"
- A decision is deferred pending more thought
- A line of inquiry has its own arc — opening question, accumulating sub-questions, eventual conclusion

Don't open a click for every exchange. One-off questions, quick clarifications, and routine operational work don't need one.

## Creating a click

    nsg click create --goal "How should we handle X?" [--parent-id <id>]

- The `--goal` is the question or inquiry — frame it as a question when possible.
- Use `--parent-id` to nest under an existing click (sub-question decomposition).
- The click starts in `live` status.

### Vocabulary discovery habit

When opening any new click, scan `docs/future/guild-vocabulary.md` for related terms and consider creating cross-links. The vocabulary tome holds latent metaphor concepts that imply future features; the only mechanism that surfaces them at the right moment is this manual habit.

Here's the proposed shape before I write it out:

### Proposed skill: `.claude/skills/briefs/SKILL.md`

**Frontmatter description:**
> Draft, refine, and post pre-Astrolabe briefs. Invoke when asked to draft, write, refine, or post a brief / commission.

**Section outline:**

1. **What a brief is** — one paragraph framing: pre-Astrolabe artifact, captures intent + non-negotiables, the input to a sage commission. Includes the role-chain table (brief → spec → code).
2. **Content discipline** — the meat of it. Includes:
   - What briefs are *for* (intent, locked-in decisions, scope fences, references to source clicks)
   - What briefs do **not** include (file:line refs, exact API signatures, full schemas, numbered §1/§2 implementation plans, table-stakes constraints, file-existence exit criteria)
   - The nuanced *may* cases — scenarios-to-verify lists and behavioral exit criteria, framed as intent not implementation
   - The "is this design intent or am I doing the spec's job?" gut check
3. **Workflow**
   - Draft in `.scratch/brief-<slug>.md`
   - Iterate with Sean inline — he'll annotate the file directly
   - Restate Sean's feedback in chat (transcript-capture habit)
   - Click references are fine — sages have click access, will resolve them when generating the spec
4. **Posting**
   - `bin/commission.sh --codex <codex> [--complexity N] -- @<path-to-brief>`
   - Title extracted from the brief's first heading
   - Complexity is the patron's dispatch-time estimate (Fibonacci 1/2/3/5/8/13/21); if Sean doesn't volunteer one, ask
5. **Post-dispatch bookkeeping**
   - Conclude the parent design click with the dispatched writ id
   - Delete the scratch file (per the existing "Collaborating on Documents" directive)
   - Coco-log entry on commit

### Proposed change to `coco.md`

Replace the in-progress brief-content addition I was going to make under "Collaborating on Documents" with a one-liner pointer, mirroring how the clicks skill is referenced:

> **### Briefs**
>
> When asked to draft, write, refine, or post a brief / commission, invoke the **briefs skill** (`.claude/skills/briefs/SKILL.md`) for the content discipline and posting workflow. Briefs are a specific kind of `.scratch/`-drafted document with their own conventions; the skill captures both the writing register and the dispatch commands.

---

Sound right? Two specific things I'd like your read on before I write it:

1. **Is "briefs" the right skill name?** Alternatives: `briefs-and-commissions`, `commission`, `pre-spec-briefs`. I lean `briefs` — short, matches the existing single-noun pattern (`clicks`, `wrap-up`).
2. **Should the skill cover the post-dispatch bookkeeping (concluding the click, deleting scratch)?** Or leave that to the existing instructions (clicks skill + collaborating-on-documents section) and just have the briefs skill focus on content + posting? I lean *include* — the bookkeeping is part of the workflow and centralizing it in the skill makes the whole "post a brief" operation legible in one place. But there's a duplication argument too.

message.content: 1 - briefs is good; 2 - yes

input.content:
---
description: Draft, refine, and post pre-Astrolabe briefs. Invoke when asked to draft, write, refine, or post a brief / commission.
---

# Briefs — Drafting and Dispatching Workflow

A **brief** is a pre-Astrolabe artifact that captures the *intent* of a unit of work and the *non-negotiable decisions* that constrain how it gets implemented. Briefs are the input to a sage commission; the sage (Astrolabe) consumes the brief and produces a spec, which the implementing artificer turns into code.

## Role chain

| Artifact | Author | Audience | Carries |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brief | Coco (with patron) | The sage (Astrolabe) | Design intent + non-negotiable decisions + scope fences + references to source clicks |
| Spec | The sage | The implementing artificer | Implementation plan, file-level changes, API signatures, test plan |
| Code | The artificer | Future agents reading the codebase | The actual implementation |

A brief that drifts into spec territory leaks decisions that should have been left to the implementer's judgment, makes the brief brittle to refactoring (file:line refs go stale), and collapses the role boundary that the guild's organization depends on. **The discipline is intent, not how-to.**

## Content discipline

### What a brief carries

- **Intent** — what this unit of work accomplishes, framed as motivation + design summary.
- **Non-negotiable decisions** — the design choices the implementer cannot revisit. Each should be the conclusion of a click (or set of clicks) from the design subtree.
- **Scope fences** — explicit out-of-scope items, especially ones the implementer might naturally drift into.
- **References to source clicks** — sages have click access and will resolve references to inline the substantive content when generating the spec. Click ids in briefs are fine; click ids in *specs* are not (specs are consumed by the artificer, who has no click access).

### What a brief does not carry

- **File paths and line numbers.** "In `clerk.ts:247-264`, replace X with Y" — that's the spec's job. The sage will figure out the right files and lines from the intent.
- **Exact API signatures or full type definitions.** A short shape sketch is fine when it's the cleanest way to express intent; full code blocks belong to the spec.
- **Numbered §1/§2/§3 implementation plans.** Briefs organize around design topics and decisions, not implementation steps.
- **Test file paths, helper names, mock setup, or assertion mechanics.**
- **Exit criteria that name specific files** ("`docs/architecture/foo.md` exists") — bake the artifact into the design intent if it matters, otherwise let the sage decide where it lives.
- **Commodity constraints** ("lint passes," "all tests pass") — those are always true and add noise.
- **Exit criteria that restate the design section verbatim** — redundant.

### The nuanced cases

Some content sits between intent and implementation. Apply the *gut check*: **is this design intent, or am I doing the spec's job?**

**Scenarios to verify** — briefs **may** include a list of behavioral cases that must hold ("writ in a 3-cycle transitions to `stuck` with all members listed"; "two failed blockers gets a resolution naming both short-ids"). Frame these as *behavioral cases the design depends on*, never as test prescriptions ("add a test in `spider.test.ts` that mocks…"). Especially valuable when the design conversation surfaced non-obvious edges (cycles, recovery paths, composition rules) that the implementer might not naturally surface. For simple briefs where the scenarios are obvious from the design text, omit.

**Behavioral exit criteria** — briefs **may** include a list of "after this lands, X must be true" checks when the design has multiple parts and a checklist helps confirm coverage. Frame these as *observable outcomes*, never as artifact-existence checks. If the brief has only a few decisions, the design section already implies the outcomes and the checklist is redundant.

The line is intent vs how-to, not test/no-test or criteria/no-criteria.

## Workflow

### Drafting

Draft in `.scratch/brief-<slug>.md`. The slug should be short and recognizable (e.g., `brief-spider-follows-gating.md`). This gives Sean a navigable file he can annotate and review in his editor — drafts are not collaborated inline in chat.

A typical brief structure (adapt as needed; this is a starting template, not a constraint):

```
# <Title>

## Intent
<one or two paragraphs: what this commission accomplishes, the gist of the design>

## Motivation
<why now, what's broken or missing, who/what is the natural consumer>

## Non-negotiable decisions
<organized by decision, not by file. Each section is one design choice
with a brief rationale. Reference the source click for each.>

## Out of scope
<explicit fences: things the implementer might drift into, with a
one-line reason for each exclusion>

## References
<the design subtree (parent click), supporting clicks, prior briefs
this one builds on or supersedes>
```

Other sections appear when the work warrants them — e.g., "Substrate changes" when the brief carries adjustments to existing substrate alongside the main work; "Authoring surfaces" when there are multiple consumer-facing entry points.

### Iteration

Sean iterates on the brief by editing the scratch file directly (or in chat). When he provides feedback — file edits, annotations, or out-of-band comments — **restate a summary of that feedback in chat**, using Sean's direct words as much as possible. This "states it for the record" so the substance appears in the transcript where Scribe can capture it (transcript-capture habit, per coco.md).

### Click references in briefs

Click ids are first-class in briefs — they're how the brief connects to the design conversation. The sage extracts and inlines the substance when generating the spec. Don't strip click ids out of briefs to "make them self-contained"; that's the sage's job, not Coco's.

## Posting

Use `bin/commission.sh` from the sanctum:

    ./bin/commission.sh --codex <codex> [--complexity N] -- @.scratch/brief-<slug>.md

- `--codex` is required. Common values: `nexus` (the framework). Ask Sean if uncertain.
- `--complexity` is the patron's dispatch-time estimate on the Fibonacci scale (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21). If Sean hasn't volunteered one when he says "dispatch this," ask before posting — it's a primary data point for X008 and missing-at-dispatch entries become a cleanup task at the next session.
- The `@<path>` form reads the body from a file. The title is auto-extracted from the brief's first heading.
- The script returns the writ id (`w-…`) on success. Capture it for the post-dispatch bookkeeping below.

Underlying CLI: `bin/commission.sh` wraps `nsg commission-post` and additionally patches the complexity into the Laboratory's commission log entry. **Always** prefer the wrapper — calling `nsg commission-post` directly skips the log-patching step.

## Post-dispatch bookkeeping

When a brief is dispatched, three things must follow:

1. **Conclude the parent design click.** The click whose subtree drove the design is now resolved by the act of dispatch. Conclude it with a short summary of the final shape and the dispatched writ id. Example conclusion:

       Design fully resolved and dispatched as commission w-mo35s0fo-1a1e3cd285bc.
       Final shape: <one-paragraph summary of the locked-in design>.

2. **Delete the scratch file.** The brief has been published into the writ system; the scratch copy is no longer the source of truth. Per the "Collaborating on Documents" directive in coco.md, scratch files are deleted when their content is published. Don't let `.scratch/` accumulate stale dispatched briefs.

3. **Coco-log entry.** The dispatch is part of a session of work; the coco-log entry covers the design conversation and the act of dispatching. Reference the writ id in the log so future sessions can join the design session to the resulting commission.

## Common pitfalls

- **Drifting into spec.** The most common failure. Symptoms: file paths appearing in the brief, full TypeScript code blocks beyond a shape sketch, test file names, exit criteria like "the foo.ts file at line N is updated." When you catch this, ask the gut-check question and rewrite.
- **Stripping click references.** Don't try to make briefs self-contained by inlining the substance of their source clicks — that's the sage's job. Briefs reference; specs inline.
- **Forgetting the complexity rating.** Missing-at-dispatch complexity becomes a session-startup cleanup task. Ask before posting.
- **Skipping the wrapper script.** `nsg commission-post` works but skips the log-patching step. Always go through `bin/commission.sh`.

toolUseResult.content:
---
description: Draft, refine, and post pre-Astrolabe briefs. Invoke when asked to draft, write, refine, or post a brief / commission.
---

# Briefs — Drafting and Dispatching Workflow

A **brief** is a pre-Astrolabe artifact that captures the *intent* of a unit of work and the *non-negotiable decisions* that constrain how it gets implemented. Briefs are the input to a sage commission; the sage (Astrolabe) consumes the brief and produces a spec, which the implementing artificer turns into code.

## Role chain

| Artifact | Author | Audience | Carries |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brief | Coco (with patron) | The sage (Astrolabe) | Design intent + non-negotiable decisions + scope fences + references to source clicks |
| Spec | The sage | The implementing artificer | Implementation plan, file-level changes, API signatures, test plan |
| Code | The artificer | Future agents reading the codebase | The actual implementation |

A brief that drifts into spec territory leaks decisions that should have been left to the implementer's judgment, makes the brief brittle to refactoring (file:line refs go stale), and collapses the role boundary that the guild's organization depends on. **The discipline is intent, not how-to.**

## Content discipline

### What a brief carries

- **Intent** — what this unit of work accomplishes, framed as motivation + design summary.
- **Non-negotiable decisions** — the design choices the implementer cannot revisit. Each should be the conclusion of a click (or set of clicks) from the design subtree.
- **Scope fences** — explicit out-of-scope items, especially ones the implementer might naturally drift into.
- **References to source clicks** — sages have click access and will resolve references to inline the substantive content when generating the spec. Click ids in briefs are fine; click ids in *specs* are not (specs are consumed by the artificer, who has no click access).

### What a brief does not carry

- **File paths and line numbers.** "In `clerk.ts:247-264`, replace X with Y" — that's the spec's job. The sage will figure out the right files and lines from the intent.
- **Exact API signatures or full type definitions.** A short shape sketch is fine when it's the cleanest way to express intent; full code blocks belong to the spec.
- **Numbered §1/§2/§3 implementation plans.** Briefs organize around design topics and decisions, not implementation steps.
- **Test file paths, helper names, mock setup, or assertion mechanics.**
- **Exit criteria that name specific files** ("`docs/architecture/foo.md` exists") — bake the artifact into the design intent if it matters, otherwise let the sage decide where it lives.
- **Commodity constraints** ("lint passes," "all tests pass") — those are always true and add noise.
- **Exit criteria that restate the design section verbatim** — redundant.

### The nuanced cases

Some content sits between intent and implementation. Apply the *gut check*: **is this design intent, or am I doing the spec's job?**

**Scenarios to verify** — briefs **may** include a list of behavioral cases that must hold ("writ in a 3-cycle transitions to `stuck` with all members listed"; "two failed blockers gets a resolution naming both short-ids"). Frame these as *behavioral cases the design depends on*, never as test prescriptions ("add a test in `spider.test.ts` that mocks…"). Especially valuable when the design conversation surfaced non-obvious edges (cycles, recovery paths, composition rules) that the implementer might not naturally surface. For simple briefs where the scenarios are obvious from the design text, omit.

**Behavioral exit criteria** — briefs **may** include a list of "after this lands, X must be true" checks when the design has multiple parts and a checklist helps confirm coverage. Frame these as *observable outcomes*, never as artifact-existence checks. If the brief has only a few decisions, the design section already implies the outcomes and the checklist is redundant.

The line is intent vs how-to, not test/no-test or criteria/no-criteria.

## Workflow

### Drafting

Draft in `.scratch/brief-<slug>.md`. The slug should be short and recognizable (e.g., `brief-spider-follows-gating.md`). This gives Sean a navigable file he can annotate and review in his editor — drafts are not collaborated inline in chat.

A typical brief structure (adapt as needed; this is a starting template, not a constraint):

```
# <Title>

## Intent
<one or two paragraphs: what this commission accomplishes, the gist of the design>

## Motivation
<why now, what's broken or missing, who/what is the natural consumer>

## Non-negotiable decisions
<organized by decision, not by file. Each section is one design choice
with a brief rationale. Reference the source click for each.>

## Out of scope
<explicit fences: things the implementer might drift into, with a
one-line reason for each exclusion>

## References
<the design subtree (parent click), supporting clicks, prior briefs
this one builds on or supersedes>
```

Other sections appear when the work warrants them — e.g., "Substrate changes" when the brief carries adjustments to existing substrate alongside the main work; "Authoring surfaces" when there are multiple consumer-facing entry points.

### Iteration

Sean iterates on the brief by editing the scratch file directly (or in chat). When he provides feedback — file edits, annotations, or out-of-band comments — **restate a summary of that feedback in chat**, using Sean's direct words as much as possible. This "states it for the record" so the substance appears in the transcript where Scribe can capture it (transcript-capture habit, per coco.md).

### Click references in briefs

Click ids are first-class in briefs — they're how the brief connects to the design conversation. The sage extracts and inlines the substance when generating the spec. Don't strip click ids out of briefs to "make them self-contained"; that's the sage's job, not Coco's.

## Posting

Use `bin/commission.sh` from the sanctum:

    ./bin/commission.sh --codex <codex> [--complexity N] -- @.scratch/brief-<slug>.md

- `--codex` is required. Common values: `nexus` (the framework). Ask Sean if uncertain.
- `--complexity` is the patron's dispatch-time estimate on the Fibonacci scale (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21). If Sean hasn't volunteered one when he says "dispatch this," ask before posting — it's a primary data point for X008 and missing-at-dispatch entries become a cleanup task at the next session.
- The `@<path>` form reads the body from a file. The title is auto-extracted from the brief's first heading.
- The script returns the writ id (`w-…`) on success. Capture it for the post-dispatch bookkeeping below.

Underlying CLI: `bin/commission.sh` wraps `nsg commission-post` and additionally patches the complexity into the Laboratory's commission log entry. **Always** prefer the wrapper — calling `nsg commission-post` directly skips the log-patching step.

## Post-dispatch bookkeeping

When a brief is dispatched, three things must follow:

1. **Conclude the parent design click.** The click whose subtree drove the design is now resolved by the act of dispatch. Conclude it with a short summary of the final shape and the dispatched writ id. Example conclusion:

       Design fully resolved and dispatched as commission w-mo35s0fo-1a1e3cd285bc.
       Final shape: <one-paragraph summary of the locked-in design>.

2. **Delete the scratch file.** The brief has been published into the writ system; the scratch copy is no longer the source of truth. Per the "Collaborating on Documents" directive in coco.md, scratch files are deleted when their content is published. Don't let `.scratch/` accumulate stale dispatched briefs.

3. **Coco-log entry.** The dispatch is part of a session of work; the coco-log entry covers the design conversation and the act of dispatching. Reference the writ id in the log so future sessions can join the design session to the resulting commission.

## Common pitfalls

- **Drifting into spec.** The most common failure. Symptoms: file paths appearing in the brief, full TypeScript code blocks beyond a shape sketch, test file names, exit criteria like "the foo.ts file at line N is updated." When you catch this, ask the gut-check question and rewrite.
- **Stripping click references.** Don't try to make briefs self-contained by inlining the substance of their source clicks — that's the sage's job. Briefs reference; specs inline.
- **Forgetting the complexity rating.** Missing-at-dispatch complexity becomes a session-startup cleanup task. Ask before posting.
- **Skipping the wrapper script.** `nsg commission-post` works but skips the log-patching step. Always go through `bin/commission.sh`.
- **Forgetting to conclude the parent click.** Leaves the design click sitting in `live` indefinitely, pretending there's still active design work when the work is actually in flight as a commission.

content.text:
Skill landed, pointer added to coco.md, committed and pushed.

# Session Summary

## What we did

- Completed the Spider dispatch-gating design subtree; dispatched commission `w-mo35s0fo-1a1e3cd285bc`.
- Established a K8s-style status convention for runtime objects (first consumer: Spider).
